title: Arrays
description: Lists that can never grow or shrink.

slides.title: Grouping Variables 📦
slides.content:
## Too Many Variables

If you want to store 5 high scores, you could create 5 variables: `score1`, `score2`, etc. But what if you have 10,000 scores?

An **Array** allows you to store a numbered list of items of the *same type* under a single variable name.

quizData.question: What is an Array?

options.text: A function that returns multiple outputs
A numbered list of items sharing the exact same type
A mixed list of text and numbers

quizData.explanation: Arrays collect items of a homogeneous type neatly under one identifier.

slides.title: Fixed in Stone 🗿
slides.content:
In Go, arrays have a very strict rule: **Their length is fixed.** 
If you create an array to hold 3 items, it can never hold 4. It can never shrink to 2.

quizData.question: What is the defining characteristic of an Array in Go?

options.text: Its length is strictly fixed at creation

quizData.explanation: Go arrays are static and cannot be resized once declared, making them very fast but rigid.

quizData.question: To format the first item in an array called `colors`, you write `colors[1]`.
quizData.explanation: False. Arrays in Go (and almost all programming languages) are 0-indexed. The first item is `colors[0]`.
